# BCMA-Directed Therapies

B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA), a member of the tumor necrosis factor receptor superfamily expressed ubiquitously on mature plasma cells and myeloma cells, has emerged as the most validated immunotherapeutic target in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma (RRMM) [@van2021Multiple]. Three distinct modalities now exploit BCMA for therapeutic benefit: chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapies, bispecific T-cell-engaging antibodies, and antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs). Together, these agents have reshaped the treatment landscape for patients with triple-class-exposed and triple-class-refractory disease.

## CAR T-Cell Therapies

### Idecabtagene Vicleucel (Ide-cel)

Idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel, bb2121) is a BCMA-directed CAR T-cell product incorporating a 4-1BB costimulatory domain. Early clinical activity was demonstrated in the phase 1 CRB-401 study, where 33 patients with RRMM achieved an objective response rate (ORR) of 85%, including complete responses (CR) in 45%. Median PFS was 11.8 months (95% CI, 6.2-17.8), and all evaluable responders achieved MRD-negative status [@Raje2019AntiBCMA]. Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) occurred in 76% of patients, though the majority (70%) were grade 1-2, with grade 3 CRS in only 6%. Neurotoxic effects occurred in 42%, predominantly grade 1-2.

The pivotal phase 2 KarMMa trial enrolled 140 patients who had received at least three prior regimens including a proteasome inhibitor, an IMiD, and an anti-CD38 antibody; 128 received ide-cel infusion. At a median follow-up of 13.3 months, the ORR was 73% (94/128), with CR or better in 33% (42/128). MRD-negative status was achieved in 26% of treated patients. Median PFS was 8.8 months across all dose levels [@Munshi2021Idecabtagene]. Grade 3-4 hematologic toxicities were nearly universal, including neutropenia (89%), anemia (60%), and thrombocytopenia (52%). CRS occurred in 84% of patients, with grade 3 or higher events in 5%.

The randomized phase 3 KarMMa-3 trial compared ide-cel with standard regimens in 386 patients with RRMM who had received two to four prior regimens. At a median follow-up of 18.6 months, ide-cel achieved a median PFS of 13.3 months versus 4.4 months for standard therapy (HR 0.49; 95% CI, 0.38-0.65; P < 0.001). The ORR was 71% versus 42% (P < 0.001), with CR rates of 39% versus 5%. Among patients who received ide-cel (n = 225), CRS occurred in 88% (grade 3 or higher in 5%) and neurotoxic effects in 15% (grade 3 or higher in 3%) [@RodriguezOtero2023Idecel]. These data established ide-cel as the first CAR T-cell therapy to demonstrate superiority over standard of care in a randomized trial for RRMM.

### Ciltacabtagene Autoleucel (Cilta-cel)

Ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel) is a BCMA-targeting CAR T-cell therapy featuring two BCMA-binding single-domain antibodies (nanobodies), which confer high-avidity bivalent binding. The phase 1b/2 CARTITUDE-1 study enrolled 113 patients with heavily pretreated RRMM (median six prior lines), of whom 97 received cilta-cel at the recommended phase 2 dose. The ORR was 98% (95/97), with stringent complete response (sCR) in 83%. At the two-year follow-up, median PFS was 27.8 months and median OS was not reached. MRD-negativity was achieved in 92% of evaluable patients [@Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene; @Martin2022Ciltacabtagene]. CRS occurred in 95% of patients (grade 3-4 in 4%), and neurotoxicity occurred in 21% (grade 3-4 in 9%), including delayed neurotoxicity events with parkinsonian features that emerged as a distinctive safety signal.

The randomized phase 3 CARTITUDE-4 trial compared cilta-cel with standard care (pomalidomide-bortezomib-dexamethasone or daratumumab-pomalidomide-dexamethasone) in 419 patients with lenalidomide-refractory myeloma after one to three prior lines. At a median follow-up of 15.9 months, median PFS was not reached in the cilta-cel group versus 11.8 months for standard care (HR 0.26; 95% CI, 0.18-0.38; P < 0.001). PFS at 12 months was 75.9% versus 48.6%, establishing cilta-cel as a transformative option even in earlier relapse settings [@SanMiguel2023Ciltacel]. Real-world data have further confirmed the feasibility of outpatient cilta-cel administration, with similar effectiveness and safety outcomes compared with inpatient delivery while significantly reducing healthcare resource utilization [@Janakiram2026Realworld].

## Bispecific T-Cell-Engaging Antibodies

### Teclistamab

Teclistamab is a first-in-class bispecific antibody that simultaneously engages CD3 on T cells and BCMA on myeloma cells, redirecting T-cell cytotoxicity against myeloma. The phase 1-2 MajesTEC-1 trial enrolled 165 patients with RRMM (median five prior lines; 77.6% triple-class-refractory) who received subcutaneous teclistamab at 1.5 mg/kg weekly. At a median follow-up of 14.1 months, the ORR was 63.0%, with CR or better in 39.4% (65/165). Among responders, 26.7% achieved MRD-negative status. The median PFS was 11.3 months (95% CI, 8.8-17.1), and the median duration of response was 18.4 months [@Moreau2022Teclistamab]. CRS occurred in 72.1% of patients (grade 3 in only 0.6%; no grade 4 events). However, infections were notably frequent (76.4%; grade 3-4 in 44.8%), reflecting the profound immunosuppression associated with prolonged T-cell redirection. Neutropenia (grade 3-4 in 64.2%) and anemia (grade 3-4 in 37.0%) were the most common hematologic toxicities.

### Elranatamab

Elranatamab, another BCMAxCD3 bispecific antibody, was evaluated in the phase 2 MagnetisMM-3 trial in patients with triple-class-refractory RRMM. Among 123 evaluable patients (median five prior lines), the ORR was 61.0%, with CR or better in 35.0%. The median duration of response was not reached at a median follow-up of 14.7 months. CRS occurred in 58% of patients (grade 3 in 0.8%), and infections occurred in 69.1% (grade 3-4 in 29.3%) [@Lesokhin2023Elranatamab]. The off-the-shelf availability and subcutaneous administration of bispecific antibodies confer practical advantages over CAR T-cell therapies, eliminating the need for leukapheresis, manufacturing time, and lymphodepleting chemotherapy.

## Antibody-Drug Conjugate: Belantamab Mafodotin

Belantamab mafodotin (belamaf) is a BCMA-targeting ADC conjugated to the microtubule inhibitor monomethyl auristatin F (MMAF) via a non-cleavable linker. The DREAMM-2 trial enrolled 196 patients with RRMM (refractory to IMiDs, PIs, and anti-CD38 antibodies) randomized to 2.5 mg/kg (n = 97) or 3.4 mg/kg (n = 99) administered every three weeks. At the 2.5 mg/kg dose, the ORR was 31% (97.5% CI, 20.8-42.6), with VGPR or better in 18%. At the 3.4 mg/kg dose, the ORR was 34% (97.5% CI, 23.9-46.0) [@Lonial2020Belantamab]. The signature toxicity of belamaf is corneal keratopathy, occurring as grade 3-4 events in 27% (2.5 mg/kg) and 21% (3.4 mg/kg) of patients, necessitating routine ophthalmologic monitoring and dose modifications. Thrombocytopenia (grade 3-4 in 20-33%) and anemia (grade 3-4 in 20-25%) were also common. Although belamaf was initially withdrawn from the market following the DREAMM-3 confirmatory trial, subsequent positive results from the DREAMM-7 and DREAMM-8 combination studies have renewed interest in its clinical application [@Nguyen2023Mechanisms].

## Non-BCMA Bispecific Antibody: Talquetamab (GPRC5D)

Talquetamab, a bispecific antibody targeting GPRC5D (G protein-coupled receptor, class C, group 5, member D) and CD3, represents a critical non-BCMA immunotherapy for RRMM. In the phase 1 MonumenTAL-1 study, 232 patients with heavily pretreated RRMM (median six prior lines) received talquetamab. At the two recommended phase 2 doses (405 mcg/kg weekly, n = 30; 800 mcg/kg every two weeks, n = 44), the ORR was 70% (95% CI, 51-85) and 64% (95% CI, 48-78), respectively. The median duration of response was 10.2 months and 7.8 months [@Chari2022Talquetamab]. CRS occurred in 77-80% (nearly all grade 1-2). Unique toxicities related to GPRC5D expression in non-malignant tissues included skin-related events (67-70%), dysgeusia (57-63%), and nail changes, which are manageable but impact quality of life. Talquetamab provides a mechanistically distinct option for patients who have failed BCMA-directed therapies and addresses the emerging challenge of BCMA antigen loss as a resistance mechanism [@Samur2021Biallelic].

## Resistance Mechanisms and Future Directions

Resistance to BCMA-directed therapies is an area of active investigation. Samur and colleagues reported a case of biallelic BCMA loss, acquired through deletion of one allele and an early stop codon on the second, as a mechanism of resistance to BCMA-targeting CAR T cells [@Samur2021Biallelic]. Additional resistance mechanisms include antigen downregulation without complete loss, T-cell exhaustion within the immunosuppressive myeloma microenvironment, and impaired CAR T-cell persistence [@Cappell2023Longterm]. Pre-CAR-T patient-reported outcomes, including quality of life and physical symptom burden, have also emerged as prognostic factors for clinical outcomes including CRS risk, neurotoxicity, and overall survival [@Mateo2026Association]. These findings underscore the need for combinatorial strategies, dual-targeting approaches, and sequential use of therapies directed at distinct antigens to overcome resistance and extend the durability of immunotherapeutic responses in RRMM.
